{

  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a 31-year-old waitress in Milan, married with three kids and juggling part-time work, I see social media as a fun escape a few times a week, but fake content really bugs me. With my high extraversion, I love chatting with friends online, but my low conscientiousness means I don't always dig deep into what I see. Fake stuff, like those wild health tips or exaggerated news, can spread fast and mess with people's heads, especially when you're busy with family and work. To handle it, I keep it simple: I check sources before sharing, like looking up a news site or asking my husband, who's more into that stuff. My moderate openness helps me stay curious but cautious, so I don't just believe everything. I also use social media's report buttons when something seems off, and I talk to my kids about not trusting everything online—they're growing up with this, so it's important. My low agreeableness means I'm not afraid to question things, even if it ruffles feathers in group chats. Honestly, dealing with fake content is about staying alert without getting stressed out; my moderate neuroticism keeps me balanced there. It's a shared responsibility—platforms should do more, but we users need to think twice before clicking. For me, it's part of keeping social media a positive space for connecting, not confusing.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a 31-year-old waitress in Milan, married with three kids and juggling part-time work, I see social media as a fun escape a few times a week, but fake content really bugs me. With my high extraversion, I love chatting with friends online, but my low conscientiousness means I don't always dig deep into what I see. Fake stuff, like those wild health tips or exaggerated news, can spread fast and mess with people's heads, especially when you're busy with family and work. To handle it, I keep it simple: I check sources before sharing, like looking up a news site or asking my husband, who's more into that stuff. My moderate openness helps me stay curious but cautious, so I don't just believe everything. I also use social media's report buttons when something seems off, and I talk to my kids about not trusting everything online—they're growing up with this, so it's important. My low agreeableness means I'm not afraid to question things, even if it ruffles feathers in group chats. Honestly, dealing with fake content is about staying alert without getting stressed out; my moderate neuroticism keeps me balanced there. It's a shared responsibility—platforms should do more, but we users need to think twice before clicking. For me, it's part of keeping social media a positive space for connecting, not confusing.

Overall, opinion emphasizes simple, personal strategies aligned with daily life and traits.
